test_id_1.query: Compare Medicaid versus provider manual guidance for dual eligible pharmacy claims.
test_id_1.answer: {"headline_summary": "According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, provider manual guidance is more up to date overall for dual eligible pharmacy claims and should receive more weight by default. The newest policy source dated 2024-12-01 is older and mainly points providers back to the eMedNY pharmacy billing guides rather than stating the dual-eligible pharmacy claim rule itself [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18,21-23 — 2025-10-01] [New York State Medicaid Update December 2024 Volume 40 Number 13:4 — 2024-12-01].", "policy_definition": "The newest policy excerpt does not define the dual-eligible pharmacy claim rule directly; it says, \"Practitioners should refer to the associated eMedNY provider manual web pages provided above, for additional program policy and billing guidance.\" [New York State Medicaid Update December 2024 Volume 40 Number 13:4 — 2024-12-01] Earlier policy guidance says, \"Pursuant to federal law, Medicaid is the payer of last resort, which means Medicaid will make payments only after all other sources of reimbursement have been exhausted. Therefore, potential third-party reimbursement sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid.\" [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — 2023-02-01] It also states, \"Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members. Dual eligible members will continue to access immunization services through Medicare.\" [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — 2021-08-01]", "provider_manual_definition": "The newest provider manual guidance is more specific for dual eligible pharmacy claims: \"The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee, no additional payment will be made. Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full.\" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01] It also says, \"The New York State (NYS) Medicaid program requires Medicare enrollment for pharmacies enrolling in Medicaid to ensure that Medicaid dual eligible members receive drugs and supplies from Medicaid providers enrolled in accordance with federal and State laws, and regulations,\" and \"all Medicaid enrolled pharmacy providers are still required by federal law to submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible members, regardless of CMS-460 enrollment status.\" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21-22 — 2025-10-01] The manual further states, \"Enrollment in this Medicare type is required when the pharmacy carries one or more items that would be required to be billed to Medicare on behalf of a dual-eligible member.\" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:23 — 2025-10-01]", "similarities": ["Both sources treat Medicare as primary for dual eligible pharmacy-related billing, because policy says \"potential third-party reimbursement sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid\" and the manual says pharmacies must \"submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible members\" [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — 2023-02-01] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21-22 — 2025-10-01].", "Both sources limit Medicaid payment in dual-eligible situations rather than allowing unrestricted Medicaid billing: policy says \"Medicaid is the payer of last resort\" and the manual says the \"total Medicare/Medicaid payment ... will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient\" [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — 2023-02-01] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01]."], "differences": ["The policy excerpts are broader and less operational, with the newest policy source only directing providers to the eMedNY manual for \"additional program policy and billing guidance,\" while the manual gives the actual payment and enrollment rules for dual eligible pharmacy claims [New York State Medicaid Update December 2024 Volume 40 Number 13:4 — 2024-12-01] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18,21-23 — 2025-10-01].", "The older policy excerpt specifically says, \"Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members,\" whereas the manual instead focuses on Medicare enrollment, claim submission, and payment limits for dual eligible members rather than repeating that blanket billing restriction [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — 2021-08-01] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21-23 — 2025-10-01]."]}

test_id_2.query: Compare Medicaid versus provider manual guidance for dual eligible pharmacy claims.
test_id_2.answer: {"headline_summary": "According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, the provider manual is more up to date overall for dual eligible pharmacy claims and should receive more weight by default. The newest policy source is dated 2024-12-01 and is consistent in direction, but it is older than the manual guidance. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01] [New York State Medicaid Update December 2024 Volume 40 Number 13:4 — 2024-12-01]", "policy_definition": "The newest policy excerpt says practitioners should refer to the eMedNY provider manual for additional billing guidance, and it directs providers to limit the NYS Medicaid claim amount to the actual invoice cost of the drug dosage administered and maintain documentation. Earlier policy guidance for dual eligible billing says Medicaid is the payer of last resort and that Medicare must be billed before Medicaid for dual enrollees. [New York State Medicaid Update December 2024 Volume 40 Number 13:4 — 2024-12-01] [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — 2023-02-01]", "provider_manual_definition": "The newest provider manual guidance says the total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed what the pharmacy provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient, and if Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee, no additional payment will be made; Medicare and Medicaid payment, if any, must be accepted as payment in full. It also says NYS Medicaid requires Medicare enrollment for pharmacies enrolling in Medicaid so dual eligible members receive drugs and supplies from providers enrolled under federal and State law, and that Medicaid-enrolled pharmacy providers must submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to dual eligible members. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21 — 2025-10-01] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21, 22 — 2025-10-01]", "similarities": ["Both sources require Medicare to be addressed before Medicaid for dual eligible pharmacy claims: policy says \"Medicare must be billed prior to billing Medicaid\" and the manual says Medicaid-enrolled providers must \"submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible members.\" [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — 2023-02-01] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21, 22 — 2025-10-01]", "Both sources treat Medicaid payment as limited in dual eligible situations: policy says \"Medicaid is the payer of last resort,\" while the manual caps combined Medicare/Medicaid payment at what would have been received for a Medicaid-only patient. [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — 2023-02-01] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01]"], "differences": ["The policy excerpts are more general and point readers to the provider manual for billing details, saying \"providers should refer to page 15 of the eMedNY New York State Billing Guidelines – Pharmacy\" and that practitioners should refer to the associated eMedNY provider manual web pages for additional policy and billing guidance. [New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8:9 — 2023-04-01] [New York State Medicaid Update December 2024 Volume 40 Number 13:4 — 2024-12-01]", "The provider manual gives more operational detail for dual eligible pharmacy claims, including Medicare enrollment requirements and the rule that combined Medicare/Medicaid payment cannot exceed the Medicaid-only amount, which is not stated in the policy excerpts. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21 — 2025-10-01] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01]"]}

test_id_3.query: Compare Medicaid versus provider manual guidance for dual eligible pharmacy claims.
test_id_3.answer: {"headline_summary": "According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, the provider manual is more up to date overall for dual eligible pharmacy claim guidance and should receive more weight by default. The newest policy source is dated 2024-02-01 and is older; it points providers to the pharmacy billing guide and related manual materials rather than giving the detailed dual-eligible claim rule itself.", "policy_definition": "The newest policy excerpt does not define the dual-eligible pharmacy claim rule directly; it says, \"For pharmacy claims, providers should refer to page 15 of the eMedNY New York State Billing Guidelines – Pharmacy\" and also to the ProDUR/ECCA Provider Manual [New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8:9 — 2023-04-01]. Older policy guidance states, \"Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members. Another policy excerpt says, \"Through coordination of benefits, Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted Medicaid coverable claims or will pay up to the Medicaid allowed amounts for drugs in classes specifically excluded from being covered under the third-party liability (TPL) Plan, including Medicare\" [New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13:16 — 2021-11-01].", "provider_manual_definition": "The newest provider manual guidance says, \"The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee, no additional payment will be made. Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full\" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01]. It also states, \"the New York State (NYS) Medicaid program requires Medicare enrollment for pharmacies enrolling in Medicaid to ensure that Medicaid dual eligible members receive drugs and supplies from Medicaid providers enrolled in accordance with federal and State laws, and regulations\" and that \"all Medicaid enrolled pharmacy providers are still required by federal law to submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible members\" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21-22 — 2025-10-01].", "similarities": ["Both sources treat dual eligible pharmacy claims as involving Medicare coordination rather than simple Medicaid-only billing: policy says dual eligible members \"will continue to access immunization services through Medicare\" and that Medicaid pays through coordination of benefits for certain claims [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — 2021-08-01; New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13:16 — 2021-11-01], while the manual says Medicare/Medicaid payment rules apply and Medicare-covered services for dual eligible members must be submitted and accepted [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 21-22 — 2025-10-01].", "Both sources limit Medicaid payment in dual-eligible situations: policy says Medicaid will pay \"the patient responsibility\" or \"up to the Medicaid allowed amounts\" in specified circumstances [New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13:16 — 2021-11-01], and the manual says total Medicare/Medicaid payment \"will not exceed\" what would have been paid for a Medicaid-only patient and that no additional payment is made if Medicare payment is greater [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01]."], "differences": ["The newest policy excerpt is indirect and cross-references other manuals for pharmacy claim instructions, saying providers should refer to the billing guidelines and ProDUR/ECCA manual [New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8:9 — 2023-04-01], while the newest provider manual gives the operative payment rule for dual eligible claims [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 21-22 — 2025-10-01].", "Older policy language is narrower and more categorical, stating that \"Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members\" and that dual eligible members access immunization services through Medicare [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — 2021-08-01], whereas the provider manual focuses on Medicare enrollment, claim submission, and payment limits for dual eligible members rather than saying pharmacies can only bill non-dual eligible members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 21-22 — 2025-10-01]."]}

[New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18,21,23 — 2025-10-01] [New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8:9 — 2023-04-01]", "policy_definition": "The newest policy excerpt does not define dual eligible pharmacy claim billing in detail; it says, \"For pharmacy claims, providers should refer to page 15 of the eMedNY New York State Billing Guidelines – Pharmacy\" and also references the ProDUR/ECCA manual. [New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8:9 — 2023-04-01] The older policy excerpt states that, for dual eligible enrollees, \"Medicaid is the payer of last resort,\" so \"potential third-party reimbursement sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid.\" [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — 2023-02-01]", "provider_manual_definition": "The newest provider manual says the total Medicare/Medicaid payment \"will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient,\" and if Medicare pays more than the Medicaid fee, \"no additional payment will be made\"; it also says the Medicare and Medicaid payment \"must be accepted as payment in full.\" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01] It further states that Medicaid enrolled pharmacy providers \"are still required by federal law to submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible members,\" and that NYS Medicaid requires Medicare enrollment for pharmacies enrolling in Medicaid so dual eligible members receive drugs and supplies from properly enrolled providers. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21,22 — 2025-10-01] The manual also says Medicare enrollment is required when the pharmacy carries items that \"would be required to be billed to Medicare on behalf of a dual-eligible member.\" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:23 — 2025-10-01]", "similarities": ["Both sources treat Medicare as primary before Medicaid for dual eligible pharmacy-related billing: policy says \"Medicaid is the payer of last resort\" and \"Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid,\" while the manual requires claims and assignments for Medicare covered services for dual eligible members. [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — 2023-02-01] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21 — 2025-10-01]", "Both sources tie dual eligible pharmacy billing to Medicare-related requirements rather than Medicaid-only billing: policy directs providers to billing guidance for pharmacy claims, and the manual requires Medicare enrollment and acceptance of Medicare/Medicaid payment rules for dual eligible members. [New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8:9 — 2023-04-01] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21,22,23 — 2025-10-01]"], "differences": ["The policy excerpts are limited and indirect: they say providers should refer to the eMedNY pharmacy billing guide and that Medicare must be billed before Medicaid, but they do not spell out the current payment cap or enrollment rules. [New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8:9 — 2023-04-01] [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — 2023-02-01]", "The provider manual is more specific and current: it states the Medicare/Medicaid total cannot exceed the Medicaid-only amount, no extra payment is made if Medicare pays more, payment must be accepted as payment in full, and Medicare enrollment is required for certain dual-eligible billing situations. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18,21,23 — 2025-10-01]"]}

The newest policy source dated 2024-12-01 is older and is more general, while the provider manual gives the current Medicare/Medicaid payment and enrollment rules for dual eligible pharmacy claims.", "policy_definition": "The newest policy excerpt is general and says providers should use eMedNY pharmacy billing guidance for pharmacy claims: \"For pharmacy claims, providers should refer to page 15 of the eMedNY New York State Billing Guidelines – Pharmacy\" and \"Per page 39 of the eMedNY Prospective Drug Utilization Review/Electronic Claims Capture and Adjudication ProDUR/ECCA Provider Manual\" [New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8:9 — Apr 01, 2023]. test_id_6.query: How do Medicaid and provider manual guidance define primary payer responsibility for dual-eligible pharmacy claims?
test_id_6.concept: primary payer responsibility for dual-eligible pharmacy claims
test_id_6.answer: {"headline_summary": "According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, the provider manual is more up to date overall for this issue and should receive more weight by default. Both sources say Medicaid is secondary to other liable coverage for pharmacy claims, but the provider manual adds that a responsible third party must pay first and that total Medicare/Medicaid payment cannot exceed the Medicaid-only amount. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16,18 — 2025-10-01] [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — 2025-04-01]", "policy_definition": "The newest policy guidance says NYS Medicaid providers \"are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid\" and that \"NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort\"; it also states that \"all other available resources [must] be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment.\" [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — 2025-04-01] Earlier policy language similarly says that \"Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility\" for correctly submitted Medicaid-coverable claims or pay up to Medicaid allowed amounts for certain excluded drug classes, but the excerpts do not further define dual-eligible pharmacy primary payer responsibility. [New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13:16 — 2021-11-01]", "provider_manual_definition": "The newest provider manual guidance says that \"all available resources [must] be used before Medicaid considers payment\" and that if \"there is a responsible third party who should be paying for the Medicaid member’s health benefits as primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first.\" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01] The manual also says that for Medicare/Medicaid claims, \"The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient\" and that if Medicare pays more than the Medicaid fee, \"no additional payment will be made.\" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01]", "similarities": ["Both sources place Medicaid after other liable coverage for pharmacy claims: policy says providers must bill \"applicable third parties\" before NYS Medicaid and that Medicaid is \"the payer of last resort,\" while the manual says \"all available resources\" must be used before Medicaid pays. [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — 2025-04-01] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01]", "Both sources require primary payer responsibility to be satisfied by another liable payer before Medicaid payment is considered: policy says other resources must be used before Medicaid considers payment, and the manual says a \"responsible third party\" that is the \"primary payor\" \"must pay first.\" [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — 2025-04-01] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01]"], "differences": ["The provider manual is more specific about dual-eligible pharmacy claims because it addresses Medicare/Medicaid payment limits, stating that total payment \"will not exceed\" the Medicaid-only amount and that if Medicare pays more than the Medicaid fee, \"no additional payment will be made\"; the policy excerpt does not include that payment-cap detail. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01] [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — 2025-04-01]", "The policy excerpt uses broader coordination-of-benefits language—\"applicable third parties\" and \"payer of last resort\"—while the provider manual expressly identifies a \"responsible third party\" as the \"primary payor\" who \"must pay first.\" [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — 2025-04-01] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01]"]}

[New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:3 — May 01, 2024; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025]", "Both sources require proper handling of Medicare cost-sharing on Medicaid claims: policy requires providers to “accurately report Medicare Part C cost-sharing amounts on Medicaid claims,” and the manual ties payment to Medicare/Medicaid coordination and says the combined payment “must be accepted as payment in full.” [New York State Medicaid Update October 2019 Volume 35 Number 10:5-6 — Oct 01, 2019; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025]"], "differences": ["The policy excerpt is narrower and specifically addresses Medicare Part C/Medicare Advantage co-payment and co-insurance recovery from “NYS Medicaid dually eligible individuals,” while the provider manual uses broader payment language and says the combined Medicare/Medicaid payment “must be accepted as payment in full.” [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:3 — May 01, 2024; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025]", "The provider manual adds a payment cap and no-additional-payment rule—“will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient” and “If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee, no additional payment will be made”—which is not stated in the policy excerpt. test_id_21.query: Compare Medicaid versus provider manual rules for when a pharmacy must bill Medicaid before billing Medicare.
test_id_21.concept: when a pharmacy must bill Medicaid before billing Medicare
test_id_21.answer: {"headline_summary": "According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, the pharmacy manual is more up to date overall for this issue and should receive more weight by default. The newest policy source dated 2025-04-01 and the provider manual both require billing other liable coverage before Medicaid, but the provider manual adds a specific Medicare-first rule for services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01] [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7 — 2025-04-01]", "policy_definition": "The newest policy source says NYS Medicaid providers must bill liable third parties before billing Medicaid: “NYS Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Federal regulations require that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment.” [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7 — 2025-04-01] An older 2022 Medicaid Update says the same general rule: “providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid, as Medicaid is always the payor of last resort” and “all other available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment.” [New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4:2 — 2022-04-01]", "provider_manual_definition": "The newest provider manual source is more specific for Medicare: “Pharmacies enrolled in the Medicaid Program as a billing provider are required to demonstrate participation in the Medicare Program. Medicaid pharmacy enrollment information can be accessed online at: https://www.emedny.org/info/ProviderEnrollment/index.aspx. Analogous to TPL in the section above, Medicare benefits must also be maximized prior to billing Medicaid. Services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01] The manual also states that “The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01]", "similarities": ["Both sources require Medicaid to be billed after other liable coverage/resources are used: policy says providers must bill “applicable third parties” before Medicaid and that Medicaid is “the payer of last resort,” while the manual says “Medicare benefits must also be maximized prior to billing Medicaid.” [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7 — 2025-04-01] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01]", "Both sources place Medicaid in a secondary payer role rather than first payer: policy says “all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment,” and the manual says “Services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare.” [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7 — 2025-04-01] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01]"], "differences": ["The policy source states the general coordination-of-benefits rule for all liable third parties, but it does not specifically say that Medicare must be billed first for dual-covered pharmacy services. [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7 — 2025-04-01]", "The provider manual is more specific and current on Medicare billing: it says pharmacies “are required to demonstrate participation in the Medicare Program” and that “Services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01]"]}

[Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf:27-28 — 2025-10-01] [mu_no12_jul23_pr.pdf:11 — 2023-07-01]", "Both sources show that the pharmacist may need to act when information is incomplete, either by verifying legitimacy/identity or by notifying the prescriber in the PA context. test_id_41.query: Are weight loss drugs an excluded benefit?
test_id_41.concept: weight loss drugs excluded benefit status
test_id_41.answer: {"headline_summary": "According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, the provider manual is more up to date overall for this issue and should receive more weight by default. Both sources indicate that drugs used for weight loss are not covered/excluded, so the answer is yes, weight loss drugs are an excluded benefit. test_id_46.answer: {"headline_summary": "According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, 340B drugs are reimbursed using claim-level identifiers and, for Medicaid 340B drug claims in NCPDP format, the ingredient cost field is set to the 340B acquisition cost; this newer provider manual should guide current billing unless you are asking about older policy history. The newest policy source dated 2024-02-01 is consistent that 340B claims must be identified correctly and that claims above the 340B ceiling price are denied and must be resubmitted with the correct ingredient cost. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 — 2025-10-01] [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:1 — 2024-02-01]", "policy_definition": "The newest policy excerpt says a claim submitted to NYS Medicaid that is higher than the 340B ceiling price “will be denied” and that denied claims “must be resubmitted with the correct ingredient cost.” It also states that 340B-purchased drug claims “must be submitted with claim level identifiers and at actual acquisition cost,” and that claim level identifiers are required for 340B-purchased drugs billed to NYRx, Medicaid Managed Care, or as a secondary claim. [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:1 — 2024-02-01] [New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11:6 — 2023-06-01] Older policy guidance adds that if rebates are received because of incorrect or missing claim-level identifiers, “the CE is responsible for reimbursing manufacturers the 340B discounts.” [New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13:14 — 2021-11-01]", "provider_manual_definition": "The newest provider manual excerpt states that 340B covered entities must decide whether they will use 340B drugs for Medicaid patients, because federal law prohibits duplicate discounts. For Medicaid 340B drug claims submitted in NCPDP format, the manual requires claim-level identifiers and shows the ingredient cost submitted as “340B Acquisition Cost” for Medicaid primary claims. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 — 2025-10-01] It also defines the 340B ceiling price as “the Average Manufacturer Price (AMP) reduced by the rebate percentage,” i.e., the Unit Rebate Amount (URA). [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:44 — 2025-10-01]", "similarities": ["Both sources require claim-level identification for 340B drugs and tie reimbursement to correct claim submission. The policy says 340B-purchased drug claims “must be submitted with claim level identifiers” [New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11:6 — 2023-06-01], and the provider manual says the required NCPDP fields include “420-DK, Submission Clarification Code (SCC) 20” and “423-DN, Basis of Cost Determination (BCD) 08” for Medicaid 340B drug claims [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 — 2025-10-01].", "Both sources connect reimbursement to the 340B acquisition/ceiling-price framework rather than ordinary pricing. The policy says claims must be submitted “at actual acquisition cost” and that claims above the “340B ceiling price” are denied [New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11:6 — 2023-06-01] [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:1 — 2024-02-01], while the provider manual defines the “340B ceiling price” as AMP minus the rebate percentage (URA) [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:44 — 2025-10-01]."], "differences": ["The provider manual is newer and more operational: it specifies the NCPDP billing fields and shows “409-D9 Ingredient Cost Submitted” as “340B Acquisition Cost” for Medicaid primary claims [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 — 2025-10-01], while the policy excerpts focus on compliance outcomes such as denial for claims above the ceiling price and resubmission with the correct ingredient cost [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:1 — 2024-02-01].", "The policy excerpts include a rebate-recovery consequence not stated in the provider manual excerpts: if rebates are received because of incorrect or missing claim-level identifiers, “the CE is responsible for reimbursing manufacturers the 340B discounts” [New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13:14 — 2021-11-01]. The provider manual excerpts instead emphasize duplicate-discount prohibition and claim formatting requirements [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 — 2025-10-01]."]}

[Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf:38 — Oct 01, 2025] The same manual page also states that on Medicaid 340B drug claims, the required fields include \"420-DK, Submission Clarification Code (SCC) 20,\" \"423-DN, Basis of Cost Determination (BCD) 08,\" and \"409-D9 Ingredient Cost Submitted 340B Acquisition Cost.\" [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf:38 — Oct 01, 2025]", "similarities": ["Both sources require 340B claims to be specially identified rather than treated like ordinary drug claims: policy says 340B drugs \"must be identified by appending the UD modifier\" and the manual requires claim-level identifiers and specific 340B claim fields. [mu_no8_jul22_pr.pdf:5 — Jul 01, 2022] [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf:38 — Oct 01, 2025]", "Both sources tie reimbursement to 340B-specific cost treatment: policy says claims must be submitted \"at actual acquisition cost,\" and the manual lists \"340B Acquisition Cost\" as the ingredient cost field. [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf:38 — Oct 01, 2025] [mu_no11_jun23_pr.pdf:6 — Jun 01, 2023] [mu_no8_jul22_pr.pdf:5 — Jul 01, 2022]", "The policy excerpts explicitly state a reimbursement consequence if the NDC is missing: \"NYS Medicaid will not provide reimbursement for the drug,\" while the provider manual excerpt instead focuses on duplicate-discount prohibition and required claim fields without stating that same reimbursement consequence in the provided text. Claims that are denied must be resubmitted with the correct ingredient cost.” [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:1 — Feb 01, 2024] Earlier policy guidance also says 340B drugs “must be identified by appending the UD modifier to the 340B drug line on submitted NYS Medicaid FFS-only claims” and that 340B drug claims are “submitted at the 340B acquisition cost by invoice to the provider” and “properly identified as 340B” for FFS and MMC claims. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:1 — Feb 01, 2024]", "policy_definition": "The newest policy excerpt says that if a claim submitted to NYS Medicaid is higher than the 340B ceiling price, “will be denied,” and that “Claims that are denied must be resubmitted with the correct ingredient cost.” [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:1 — Feb 01, 2024] Earlier policy guidance says “Drugs obtained via the 340B program must be identified by appending the UD modifier to the 340B drug line on submitted NYS Medicaid FFS-only claims,” and that if the NDC is not referenced, “NYS Medicaid will not provide reimbursement for the drug.” [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:5 — Jul 01, 2022] The 2019 update also states that for Medicaid FFS primary claims, system editing compares the submitted ingredient cost with the 340B ceiling price, defined as the maximum amount a manufacturer can charge a covered entity for a 340B covered outpatient drug. [New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9:6 — Aug 01, 2019]", "provider_manual_definition": "The newest provider manual excerpt says that upon enrollment in the 340B program, “340B covered entities must determine whether they will use 340B drugs for their Medicaid patients,” and that “Federal law prohibits duplicate discounts – manufacturers are not required to provide a discounted 340B price and a Medicaid drug rebate for the same drug.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 — Oct 01, 2025] The manual also defines the 340B ceiling price as “the Average Manufacturer Price (AMP) reduced by the rebate percentage, which is commonly referred to as the Unit Rebate Amount (URA).” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:44 — Oct 01, 2025]", "similarities": ["Both sources tie 340B reimbursement to the 340B ceiling price and limit payment when the submitted amount is too high: the policy says a claim above the ceiling price “will be denied,” and the manual defines the ceiling price as the AMP reduced by the rebate percentage/URA. [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:5 — Jul 01, 2022] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 — Oct 01, 2025]"], "differences": ["The policy excerpts focus on claim-level reimbursement mechanics—NDC reporting, the UD modifier, and denial/resubmission when the ingredient cost exceeds the 340B ceiling price—whereas the provider manual excerpt focuses on program participation and federal duplicate-discount restrictions. test_id_51.query: How are home infusion drugs covered, and who is responsible for billing
test_id_51.concept: home infusion drugs coverage and billing responsibilities
test_id_51.answer: {"headline_summary": "According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, provider manual guidance is more up to date overall for this issue and should receive more weight by default. The newest policy evidence is dated 2022-07-01 and is consistent on billing responsibility, but it is older and less specific about home infusion coverage than the provider manual. [Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01] [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:6 — 2022-07-01]", "policy_definition": "Policy documents show that Medicaid covers certain administered drugs when they are given by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous methods and are for FDA-approved indications or recognized off-label indications in drug compendia: \"drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous methods are covered when provided for Federal Drug Administration (FDA)-approved indications and those recognized off-label indications listed in the drug compendia\" [New York State Medicaid Update February 2019 Volume 35 Number 3:3 — 2019-02-01]. For billing responsibility, the policy says \"it is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial plan, Medicare, or Medicaid\" and also states that both the facility and the pharmacy must actively seek TPL enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for patients [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — 2020-07-01]. The newer policy excerpt also notes that Medicaid may pay patient responsibility for correctly submitted Medicaid-coverable claims or up to Medicaid allowed amounts for certain drugs excluded under TPL/Medicare coordination rules [New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13:16 — 2021-11-01].", "provider_manual_definition": "The newest provider manual says home infusion is not paid as a bundled package: \"The New York State Medicaid Program does not provide a bundled payment to cover drugs, supplies and services associated with home infusion treatments\" [Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01]. test_id_55.answer: {"headline_summary": "According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, provider manual guidance is more up to date overall for home infusion drugs and should receive more weight by default. The newest policy source dated 2022-10-01 addresses coverage for drugs administered in a practitioner’s office, while the provider manual specifically says home infusion drugs and supplies are not bundled and must be billed as pharmacy or medical benefit items.", "policy_definition": "The newest policy excerpt says NYS Medicaid FFS covers “drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous methods in the practitioner’s office” for FDA-approved indications or medically accepted indications supported by the listed compendia, and if there is no recognized indication, an approved IRB protocol may be used [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:4 — 2022-07-01]. Older policy language also states that “drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous methods are covered when provided for Federal Drug Administration (FDA)-approved indications and those recognized off-label indications listed in the drug compendia” [New York State Medicaid Update February 2019 Volume 35 Number 3:3 — 2019-02-01]. For billing responsibility, the policy excerpts say “It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial plan, Medicare, or Medicaid” and that both the facility and the pharmacy must actively seek TPL enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for patients [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — 2020-07-01].", "provider_manual_definition": "The newest provider manual guidance says, “The New York State Medicaid Program does not provide a bundled payment to cover drugs, supplies and services associated with home infusion treatments” and that “Home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01]. It also says NYS Medicaid recognizes the need for “certain drugs, including home infusion drugs, requiring administration by a health care provider” to be available through both the medical benefit and pharmacy benefit, subject to pharmacy-benefit eligibility criteria [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:12 — 2025-10-01].", "similarities": ["Both sources treat infusion-type drugs as potentially covered under Medicaid when the drug meets coverage criteria, rather than as an automatic bundled home-infusion package [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:4 — 2022-07-01; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01].", "Both sources place billing responsibility on the billing entity/provider side rather than describing a single bundled Medicaid payment for home infusion services [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — 2020-07-01; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01]."], "differences": ["The provider manual is more specific to home infusion and says there is no bundled payment and that home infusion drugs and supplies “must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit,” while the policy excerpts focus on drugs administered in a practitioner’s office and do not specifically define home infusion billing [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01; New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:4 — 2022-07-01].", "The policy excerpts assign billing responsibility to “the pharmacy” and say it must bill “timely and to the appropriate party,” whereas the provider manual excerpt does not assign the billing duty to a specific party in the quoted text [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — 2020-07-01; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01]."]}

[Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — 2025-10-01] [New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4:2-3 — 2024-04-01]", "policy_definition": "NYS Medicaid covers naloxone over the counter (OTC) when prescribed by the practitioner of a NYS Medicaid member or dispensed to a NYS Medicaid member in compliance with the State-issued naloxone standing order. [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:4-5 — 2024-02-01] When dispensing naloxone OTC via a standing order, the excerpts require that the member specifically requested the item on the date of service or the pharmacist made a pharmacist-initiated dispensing based on a determination of need/risk on DOS, that the pharmacy submit one course of therapy (two doses) with no refills, and that the item be dispensed according to FDA guidelines, NYS laws/rules/regulations, and NYS Medicaid requirements. [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:5 — 2024-02-01] The newest policy excerpt also states that for the drug, the claim must be submitted using the ordering prescriber’s NPI in the Prescriber ID field, and that the pharmacy will be reimbursed for the medication when billed via NDC using reimbursement methodology. [New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4:2-3 — 2024-04-01]", "provider_manual_definition": "The newest provider manual excerpt states that the pharmacy will be reimbursed for the medication when billed via National Drug Code (NDC) using reimbursement methodology, and that if billing for both the drug and administration, the claims must be submitted separately. [Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — 2025-10-01] It also says the drug claim must use the ordering prescriber’s NPI in the Prescriber ID field, while the administration claim must use procedure code 96372 and the pharmacy’s NPI in the Prescriber ID field. [Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — 2025-10-01]", "similarities": ["Both sources say the medication is reimbursed when billed via NDC using reimbursement methodology. [New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4:2-3 — 2024-04-01] [Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — 2025-10-01]", "Both sources require the ordering prescriber’s NPI in the Prescriber ID field for the drug claim. [New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4:2-3 — 2024-04-01] [Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — 2025-10-01]"], "differences": ["The policy excerpts add OTC/naloxone standing-order eligibility conditions, including member request or pharmacist-initiated dispensing, one course of therapy (two doses), and no refills; the provider manual excerpt does not mention these naloxone-specific conditions. [Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — 2025-10-01] [New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4:2-3 — 2024-04-01]"]}

The newest policy source dated 2024-04-01 and the provider manual both say naloxone is reimbursed when billed as a pharmacy claim, but the policy excerpt is more specific about OTC coverage while the manual excerpt is more specific about separate billing for drug and administration.", "policy_definition": "The newest policy excerpt says the pharmacy will be reimbursed for naloxone when the medication is billed via National Drug Code (NDC) using the reimbursement methodology, and it also says OTC naloxone is covered when prescribed by a practitioner or dispensed under the State-issued standing order: “The pharmacy will be reimbursed for the medication when billed via National Drug Code (NDC) using reimbursement methodology.” [New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4:2-3 — Apr 01, 2024] “New York State (NYS) Medicaid now covers naloxone over-the-counter (OTC) when either prescribed by the practitioner of a NYS Medicaid member or dispensed to a NYS Medicaid member in compliance with the State-issued naloxone standing order.” [New York State Medicaid Update September 2023 Volume 39 Number 14:7-8 — Sep 01, 2023] The older December 2022 update only identifies naloxone as “the life-saving medication that can reverse opioid overdose” and does not add reimbursement detail. [New York State Medicaid Update December 2022 Volume 38 Number 14:1 — Dec 01, 2022]", "provider_manual_definition": "The newest provider manual excerpt says naloxone-related claims are reimbursed when billed separately for the drug and administration, with the drug billed under the ordering prescriber’s NPI and reimbursed via NDC methodology: “If billing for both the drug and administration, these claims must be submitted separately.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — Oct 01, 2025] “For the drug, the claim must be submitted using the ordering prescriber’s NPI in the Prescriber ID field. The pharmacy will be reimbursed for the medication when billed via National Drug Code (NDC) using reimbursement methodology.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — Oct 01, 2025] It also says administration is billed with procedure code 96372 and the pharmacy’s NPI in the Prescriber ID field. [New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4:2-3 — Apr 01, 2024] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — Oct 01, 2025]", "Both sources tie reimbursement to pharmacy billing instructions rather than describing a separate special payment amount in the excerpted text.
